coredata.dc:title: Investigation of ultrasound-assisted starch acetylation by single- and dual- frequency ultrasound based on rheology modelling, non-isothermal reaction kinetics, and flow/acoustic simulation
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Abedi, Elahe
Roohi, Reza
Hashemi, Seyed Mohammad Bagher
Kaveh, Shima
coredata.dc:description: To achieve wheat starch acetylation (AC) with a high degree of substitution (DS), the acetylation process was carried out using various ultrasonication frequencies, including 25 kHz, 40 kHz, and 25 + 40 kHz. In the second step, wheat starch's ultrasound-assisted acetylation (UAA) is simulated using various approaches including the rheology models, non-isothermal reaction kinetics, and flow/acoustic modelling. The computational fluid dynamics (CFD) simulation solves the non-linear acoustic governing equation to determine the flow field and the amount of delivered ultrasound energy. The acetylated starch increased peak and final viscosity, with the highest values observed for the 25 + 40 kHz frequency than other single frequencies (25 kHz and 40 kHz). The viscosity of the starch is specified based on the experimental data using Herschel–Bulkley, power law, and Casson rheology models. According to differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) analysis, the gelatinization parameters and enthalpy of gelatinization (ΔHgel), were found to be lower in acetylated starches at the frequency of 25 + 40 kHz compared to those at frequencies of 25 kHz and 40 kHz, as well as native starches (NS). Moreover, the gelatinization process is examined by implementing the non-isothermal reaction kinetics to obtain the activation energy and reaction order. Based on the results obtained, implementing sonication at 25 kHz reduces the activation energy by 70.3 % compared to native starch. However, the same parameter is obtained to be 69.9 % and 67.1 % for the application of 40 and 25 + 40 kHz transducers, respectively. Additionally, during the sonication treatment, the yield shear stress increases between 24.1 and 31.8 %, based on the applied frequency. Morphology analysis determined by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) revealed that the surfaces and small granules underwent more damage in acetylated starches at frequencies of 25 kHz and 40 kHz. However, in acetylated starches at 25 + 40 kHz, the larger granules were more affected than the smaller ones.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ultrasound-assisted acetylation
Rheology modeling
Non-isothermal reaction kinetics
CFD
Dual frequency ultrasonic

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: UAA of wheat starch under different single and dual frequencies

xocs:cp-license-lines.xocs:cp-license-line: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license.

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2023 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The schematic of a) acoustic domain and boundary conditions, b) CFD domain and boundary conditions and mesh and c) acoustic grids at 25 and 40 kHz.
RVA and SEM analysis of ultrasound-assisted acetylation of wheat starch at frequency 24 + 40 kHz (A), 24 kHz (B), 40 kHz (C), and native (D) starch.
The velocity contours and vectors for the frequency of 25 kHz above the ultrasonic transducer at various time instances.
The contours of a) Total power dissipation density (W/m3) and b) Surface intensity magnitude (W/m2).
The obtained data from the DSC endotherms: a) Degree of gelatinization, b) Reaction rate.
The obtained data from the DSC endotherms: a) Degree of gelatinization, b) Reaction rate.
The utilized thermal and acoustic properties and parameters.
The power law fluid model parameters.
The reaction kinetics and statistical assessment parameters.

head.ce:title: Investigation of ultrasound-assisted starch acetylation by single- and dual- frequency ultrasound based on rheology modelling, non-isothermal reaction kinetics, and flow/acoustic simulation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Food Science and Technology, Faculty of Agriculture, Fasa University, Fasa, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Food Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Food Science and Technology, Faculty of Agriculture, Fasa University, Fasa, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Mechanical Engineering, Faculty of Engineering, Fasa University, Fasa, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Mechanical Engineering, Faculty of Engineering, Fasa University, Fasa, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: To achieve wheat starch acetylation (AC) with a high degree of substitution (DS), the acetylation process was carried out using various ultrasonication frequencies, including 25 kHz, 40 kHz, and 25 + 40 kHz. In the second step, wheat starch's ultrasound-assisted acetylation (UAA) is simulated using various approaches including the rheology models, non-isothermal reaction kinetics, and flow/acoustic modelling. The computational fluid dynamics (CFD) simulation solves the non-linear acoustic governing equation to determine the flow field and the amount of delivered ultrasound energy. The acetylated starch increased peak and final viscosity, with the highest values observed for the 25 + 40 kHz frequency than other single frequencies (25 kHz and 40 kHz). The viscosity of the starch is specified based on the experimental data using Herschel–Bulkley, power law, and Casson rheology models. According to differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) analysis, the gelatinization parameters and enthalpy of gelatinization (ΔHgel), were found to be lower in acetylated starches at the frequency of 25 + 40 kHz compared to those at frequencies of 25 kHz and 40 kHz, as well as native starches (NS). Moreover, the gelatinization process is examined by implementing the non-isothermal reaction kinetics to obtain the activation energy and reaction order. Based on the results obtained, implementing sonication at 25 kHz reduces the activation energy by 70.3 % compared to native starch. However, the same parameter is obtained to be 69.9 % and 67.1 % for the application of 40 and 25 + 40 kHz transducers, respectively. Additionally, during the sonication treatment, the yield shear stress increases between 24.1 and 31.8 %, based on the applied frequency. Morphology analysis determined by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) revealed that the surfaces and small granules underwent more damage in acetylated starches at frequencies of 25 kHz and 40 kHz. However, in acetylated starches at 25 + 40 kHz, the larger granules were more affected than the smaller ones.

ce:section.ce:para: Starch is a cost-effective, sustainable, and biodegradable polymer that is widely abundant in various sources such as cereal grains, tuber crops, legumes, fruits, leaves, and roots [1]. It plays a crucial role in various food applications due to its functional contributions in thickening, stabilizing, texturizing, gelation, encapsulation, and extending shelf life. These properties enhance the overall quality and texture of food products while also influencing their overall acceptability [2].

The modification of starch is an ongoing process that has led to the development of new processing technologies and market trends. Modified starch provides several advantages, such as improved functionality, texture, enhanced stability, product versatility, sustainability, and cost-effectiveness. The most commonly used methods for starch modification include physical, chemical, and enzymatic modifications. These methods enable the modification of starch to enhance its properties and functionality for specific applications [3].

Single modifications have proven to be effective in improving various properties of starch, including physicochemical properties, rheological properties, in-vitro starch digestibility, and mechanical strength of starch films, among others. Singly modified starch has found applications in diverse fields, such as the production of starch nanocrystals, hydrogels, and biodegradable films in both the food and non-food industries. However, there are certain limitations to their application, such as lower thermal stability, instability of hydrogels and emulsions, and reduced mechanical strength of films under specific processing conditions [4]. To address the limitations and improve the functional properties, emulsion stability, hydrogel stability, film mechanical strength, and physicochemical properties of starch, dual modification has been introduced [1]. Dual modification can also help reduce the consumption of chemicals and enzymes in the production of novel starches, while improving the efficiency of single modifications and achieving desired outcomes more quickly. There are two methods for combining two single modification processes to optimize functionality based on specific requirements: sequential modification, where the modifications are conducted one after the other, and simultaneous modification, where both modifications are carried out at the same time. These approaches offer flexibility and allow for customization of the modification process to meet specific needs [1].

The process of acetylation is a significant method of substituting starch, which is commonly utilized as an emulsifier, coating agent, and thickening agent in various applications. It offers stability and resistance to retrogradation, which means that it maintains its properties over time without undergoing undesirable changes. This makes it particularly useful in food and industrial formulations where long-term stability is required. Acetylated starch refers to a granular starch ester in which the CH3CO group is introduced at low temperatures, improving the properties of starch and making it more stable and resistant to retrogradation compared to its native form. Moreover, it increases viscosity, solubility, swelling factor, hardness, cohesiveness, adhesiveness, and translucency of gels. Additionally, it reduces the initial gelatinization temperature, making the process more efficient. These properties make acetylated starch a versatile ingredient in food and industrial applications where gel formation and texture modification are desire [5–7].

In order to enhance the degree of acetylation, various dual modifications have been applied such as ultrasonication-assisted [6,8], pulsed electric fields assisted acetylation [9,10], and ultra-high pressure -assisted acetylation [11].

Ultrasonic treatments have the potential to expedite chemical reactions and enhance yields. When subjected to sonication, the surface area of starch granules is augmented as a result of the generation of channels and/or cavities on both the outer surface and interior of the granules. Various alterations have been carried out on ultrasonication, such as the acetylation of dioscorea starch subsequent to ultrasound treatment [8], octenyl succinate starch [12], carboxymethyl starch [13], and octenylsuccinates of carboxymethyl starch [14].

During the sonication treatment several complicated and interconnected phenomena occur including the flow streaming, creation of oscillation pressure field, mass transfer between vapor and liquid phased due to creation and explosion of nanobubbles and so on. Therefore, it is essential to simulate the flow and acoustic pressure fields to determine the process specifications [15–24]. Moreover, as the viscosity of the fluid varies during the sonication treatment, a through rheology modeling of the viscosity as a function of shear rate provides valuable data regarding the flow characteristics. In addition to these two simulation types, the gelatinization procedure and the reaction progress can be mathematically modeled using non-isotherm kinetics. The kinetics study provides useful data regarding the reaction rate and the activation energy of the wheat starch's ultrasound-assisted acetylation (UAA) at various operational frequencies including 25, 40 and 25 + 40 kHz. The utilization of the several simulation approaches is significantly helpful in examination of the sonication treatment from various viewpoints.

The native wheat starch used in this study was sourced from Fars Glucosin Company located in Shiraz, Iran. In the experiment, 100 g of starch was mixed with 224 mL of distilled water and stirred for 1 h at 24 °C. The pH was adjusted to 8 using a 1 mol.L-1 NaOH solution. Acetic anhydride (6 %) was then gradually added to the suspension at pH 8. The acetylation reaction was allowed to proceed for 10 min. The suspensions were subjected to sonication using an ultrasonic bath. The sonication was performed at an amplitude of 24 % with input power of up to 400 W and working frequencies of 25 kHz, 40 kHz, and 25 + 40 kHz for a duration of 5 min. The sonication was carried out at temperatures of 60 °C. After sonication, the pH was adjusted to 4.5 using a 1 mol.L-1 HCl solution for 5 min. All experiments were conducted in beakers placed in an ultrasonic bath (Pacisa SA, Spain) equipped with a temperature-controlling system. The ultrasonic bath had internal dimensions of 300 × 150 × 150 mm3 and contained four transducers at the bottom. The resulting suspensions were thoroughly washed, lyophilized, milled using a laboratory mill (AlexanderWerck, Model WEL82, Germany), and manually sieved to obtain particle sizes smaller than 40 μm [25].

To analyze the microstructure of the modified starch, scanning electron microscopy (SEM) was performed using a Leica SEM model from Cambridge, UK. The method proposed by Pourmohammadi et al. (2018) was followed for this analysis [26].

To evaluate the effect of UAA on the pasting properties of wheat starch, a rapid visco-analyzer (RVA) from Newport Scientific Pty. Ltd., Warriewood, Australia, interfaced with a personal computer, was used. First, 4 g of dry basis NS (native wheat starch) and AC (acetylated wheat starch) starches were weighed and placed into an RVA canister. Then, 25 g of distilled water was added to each sample. The mixture was manually stirred for 30 s using the plastic RVA paddle to ensure a uniform dispersion of solids in water. The canister was attached to the RVA instrument, and the suspension was stirred at 960 rpm for the first 20 s and then at 160 rpm for the remaining duration of the experiment, which lasted for 23 min. For the heating program, the sample was initially kept at 30 °C for 1 min. Then, it was heated at a rate of 6 °C per minute until it reached 95 °C. The temperature was maintained at 95 °C for 5 min before being reduced at the same rate of 6 °C per minute back to 30 °C. The sample was then held at 30 °C for the remaining 17 min of the run. From the obtained pasting profiles, various RVA parameters were determined, including the pasting temperature, peak viscosity, final viscosity, breakdown, and setback. These parameters provide insights into the pasting properties of the starches and their behavior during heating and cooling processes [27].

The gelatinization properties of both native and hydrolyzed starches were investigated using a differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) instrument (OIT-5000, Sanaf Electronic Co., Iran) equipped with STAR software. The changes in conclusion temperature (Tc), enthalpy (ΔH in J/g of dry starch), onset temperature (To), and peak temperature (Tp) of gelatinization were determined based on the exothermic DSC curves. This analysis was conducted as described in the study by Abedi et al. [28].
In this section the governing equations including the rheology models, non-isothermal reaction kinetics and flow/acoustic laws are presented.

The rheology modeling is applied to determine the fluid viscosity and the relation between shear rate and shear rate. The flow behavior in the steady state is simulated using various rheological modeling to examine various aspects for starch viscosity in a wide range of shear rates. The incorporated models are Herschel–Bulkley, Casson and power law. It should be added that, in order to study materials behavior at low shear rates and explore the possibility of a shear rate that is not equal to zero at zero shear, it is logical to assume the existence of a yield shear stress. Therefore, the Casson and Herschel-Bulkley models are utilized to analyze this occurrence. Moreover, it can be observed that at high shear rates, the relationship between shear stress and shear rate is almost linear, indicating a Newtonian behavior within this range. The usage of the Casson flow model effectively captures this behavior. As a result, the experimental data was matched to the power law, Casson, and Herschel-Bulkley flow models.

The Herschel–Bulkley fluid model is an expansion of the Bingham model. The model is based on the experimental data to determine the non-Newtonian viscosity. The model relates the shear stress and shear rate using consistency index, the degree of shear thickening/thinning and yield stress. The utilization of the yield stress helps to capture the solid-like nature of some fluids at very low shear rates [29].
(1)
τ =
τ 0
+ K
γ ̇
n
where in the above equation,
τ
nd
τ 0
represent the stress and yield stress and the constants of K and n are consistency and flow behavior indexes, respectively.

The Casson model is a well-known model for viscosity of food and biological products. The model has two distinct behaviors at very low and high shear rates. At low shear rates the fluid behaves as a solid-like material for which an initial yield shear rate is required for fluid flow. On the other hand, at high shear rates a nearly Newtonian viscosity (
η C
) is observed [30]:
(2)
τ
=
τ 0
+
η C
γ ̇

The importance of this model is due to its simplicity and accuracy in specified ranges of shear rate. The model covers various Newtonian and non-Newtonian (shear thinning and shear thickening) fluids and widely utilized by researchers [29].
(3)
τ = K
γ ̇
n
where n is the flow index. The fitting to viscosity data is performed using the Origin Pro software.

To simulate the reaction kinetics, nth order reaction is considered. So, the gelatinization kinetics can be formulated as [31]:
(4)

where in the above equation

is the fraction of gelatinized starch,

is the reaction rate, t is the time and n are the order of implemented kinetics. The rate of gelatinization is denoted by

The gelatinization reaction progress is represented by

The reaction rate constant (k) is defined based on the Arrhenius model, which assumes an activation energy barrier for reactions as:

To simulate the ultrasonic waves transport within the bath domain the Helmholtz equation is applied as [33]:
(14)

The density, sound speed in the fluid and pressure are denoted by
ρ
,

and P. The space coordinates vector is represented by
X →

Based on the harmonic nature of the acoustic waves and the conservation of this property during interaction with domain boundaries, it is reason able to transform the Helmholtz equation into frequency domain. Implementation of the

The oscillations frequency is denoted by

[34].

The accuracy of the implemented models is statistically evaluated using R2, adjusted R2 and RMSE. The magnitude of the adjusted R2 is determined as:
(16)
Adjusted
R
2
= 1 -
[
( n - i )
( 1 -
R
2
)
n - p
]
where n is the measured data population, p is quantity of the population and i is the intercept indicator [35].

The mean difference between the predicted and observed data are calculated as:
(17)
RMSE =
( o b s e r v e d - p r e d i c t e d )
2
n - p

To simulate the pressure waves propagation within the field proper boundary conditions should be applied. To do so, at the transducers surface, the oscillating wall condition with suitable amplitude and frequency is set. The amplitude is determined based on the transducer power, material and frequency. For the peripheral side to prevent the utilization of very far boundary the perfectly matched layer (PML) in applied. At the other boundaries, the hard wall assumption is considered (Fig. 1 -a).

For the CFD simulation, at the transducer walls, the time-location dependent boundary condition is assumed. The normal velocity at those surfaces is assumed to vary sinusoidally with the frequency of the sonication treatment. The applied velocity amplitude is distributed along the transducer surface based on the distribution proposed by Rahimi, et al. [36], which offers the maximum velocity to occur at the transducer axis. The upper free surfaces are assigned as the constant pressure boundaries and the other walls are set as no-slip boundary condition (Fig. 1-b).

As the numerical simulation is performed in two phases namely, CFD and acoustic simulation, it is mandatory to implement appropriate grids for these phases. For the acoustic simulation, the propagation of the sound waves fluctuations is occurred at very high frequencies (25 and 40 kHz). So, the grid sizing should be sufficiently fine to model their propagation. Therefore, the grids’ sizing is selected to vary in the range of λ/10 to λ/6 from the zone of high gradients to farther zones. Based on the selected sizing and implementation of the grid study the optimum number of cells is determined to be around 110,987 and 210,987 for the treatments with 25 and 40 kHz frequency, respectively. The utilized grids for acoustic simulation are represented in Fig. 1-c.

For the CFD simulation the grid sizing and its density are location-dependent. In the zones with high flow field gradients, finer cells should be used with gradually size increase toward peripheral regions. As it is illustrated in Fig. 1-c, for CFD simulation the highest grid density is assigned to the vicinity of the transducers’ curves and a gradual increase with the rate of 1.06 is set for larger cells in farther locations. So, based on the grid study of CFD simulation the number of computational cells is 45,912. The utilized thermal and acoustic properties and parameters are listed in Table 1 .

For the CFD simulation the time step should be sufficiently small to be compatible to high frequency of the acoustic oscillations. As the frequency is set to 25 kHz and 40 kHz, and the requirement of at least ten-time step in each time period of oscillation the time step is to 4
×
10-6 and 2.5
×
10-6 s, respectively. The convergence criteria for each iteration are set to 10-6 for all of the governing equations. The RNG turbulent model is implemented due to its capability to simulate the curvature of streamlines and intense strain rates.

Besides, it should be noted that, the acoustic field has direct influence on the flow streaming, however the response time difference between these two fields is main reason of the implemented decupling procedure. The acoustic field fluctuates at significantly lower time intervals compared to the flow field. So, it is reasonable to utilize decouple approach in solution of acoustic and flow equations.

The coupled acoustic and CFD simulations are performed using the COMSOL MultiphysicsTM 6.2. The frequency and time domain are selected to solve the ultrasonic and flow field domains, respectively [37]. The computations are performed via a workstation with the specification of CPU: Intel® Xeon® Processor E6540 (18 M Cache), RAM: 16 GB DDR. The computational time is varied between 56 and 61 h based on the magnitude of the sonication frequency.

ce:section.ce:section-title: UAA of wheat starch under different single and dual frequencies
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2 displays the morphological patterns of NS and AC starches. The granules of wheat starch, labeled as A and B, are clearly visible. Modification through the application of ultrasonication has been observed to induce notable alterations in the morphology of starch granules. It can be showed a significant effect on the surface morphology of starch granules. The reaction between acetic anhydride and starch granules under ultrasonic conditions leads to the formation of cracks, pores, and fractures on the surface of the granules. These structural changes facilitate the penetration of chemical reagents into the granules. The starch granules showed partial disintegration and slight gelatinization when esterification reactions were performed with the presence of a catalyst, such as NaOH solution [38]. These observations indicate that the modification process, especially in the presence of a catalyst, can cause surface damage and partial disintegration of the starch granules. This may be attributed to the esterification reactions and the formation of cross-linked bonds on the granule surfaces. In a study by Bello-Pérez et al. (2010), it was found that esterification of starch resulted in increased granule fusion. This fusion is attributed to the enhanced hydrogen bonding caused by the incorporation of hydrophilic groups into the starch molecules [39]. The coalescence of starch molecules leads to the fusion of starch granules, which is consistent with the morphology observed in the SEM images. These findings align with previous research by Van Hung & Morita (2005a, b) [40,41]. Upon modification, the surfaces of all large granules in AC wheat starches at the dual frequency of 25 + 40 kHz exhibited more damage compared to the small granules. In contrast, at frequencies of 25 kHz and 40 kHz, the small granules were more affected than the large granules.

Sonification, or ultrasonic treatment, has been found to increase the DS in starch. This is attributed to the improved solubility and swelling of starch granules, which occurs as a result of enhanced contact with reagents. The increased contact improves the homogeneity of the reactants, leading to a more efficient chemical reaction. During sonification, the collapse of cavitation bubbles generated by the ultrasonic waves can cause mechanical damage and rupture of the starch granules [27,42]. This phenomenon is due to the high-pressure gradients, high local velocities of liquid layers, shearing force, and microstreaming created by the collapsing bubbles. The mechanical damage and polymer chain breaking caused by sonification contribute to the increased DS in starch. This is also because the broken chains provide more sites for the reaction to occur, leading to a higher DS. According to Amini, Razavi & Mortazavi (2015) and Zhu (2015), the use of ultrasound can create fractures, pores, and cracks on the surfaces of starch granules. This leads to an increase in the surface area of the starch particles. The enhanced surface area promotes the penetration of esterifying agents, such as acetyl groups, into the granular structure of starch, facilitating their interaction with the starch molecules. Furthermore, ultrasound can accelerate the chemical reactions involved in esterification, as the mechanical forces generated by ultrasound disrupt the starch granules and break the polymer chains, allowing for a faster reaction between the esterifying agents and starch molecules [43,44].

The frequency of ultrasound plays a crucial role in enhancing cavitational bubbles for several reasons. There are two key reasons why the frequency of ultrasound affects the enhancement of cavitational bubbles: 1) Cavitation yield decreases with increasing frequency: As stated by Abedi et al. (2017), the scattering, attenuation, and shortening of the acoustic cycle at higher frequencies contribute to a decrease in cavitation yield. This means that as the frequency of ultrasound increases, the formation and persistence of cavitational bubbles are reduced [45]. 2) Instable cavitation at low frequencies: According to Abedi et al. (2017), low frequencies can induce unstable cavitation, where the cavitational bubble collapses rapidly and violently. This intense collapse results in more rapid agitation and mass transfer [45].

The peak and final viscosity of NS starch was observed to be significantly lower (p < 0.05) compared to the AC samples at frequencies of 25 kHz, 40 kHz, and 25 + 40 kHz. For example, the peak viscosity of native starch was measured to be 3757 ± 48, whereas the acetylated starch had a higher peak viscosity of 7188 ± 32 mPa.s (at frequency of 25 kHz), 9423 ± 112 mPa.s (at frequency of 25 + 40 kHz) and 5846 ± 65 mPa.s (at frequency of 40 kHz). Similarly, the final viscosity of native starch was 7034 ± 34, while the acetylated starch had a higher final viscosity (mPa.s) around 8851 ± 31, 7648 ± 66, and 11174 ± 131 at frequencies of 25 kHz, 40 kHz and 25 + 40 kHz, respectively.

The ACs at a frequency of 25 + 40 kHz (50.9 ± 1.2 °C) exhibited lower pasting temperatures compared to the starch samples at frequencies of 25 kHz (58.3 ± 1.4 °C), 40 kHz (64.4 ± 1.2 °C), and the native starch (71.6 ± 1.6 °C). The findings mentioned in this study is align with other studies conducted by Mirmoghtadaie et al. (2009), Mendoza et al. (2016), Hong et al. (2016a, b), Li et al. (2014) and Olagunju et al. (2020) which the modification of starch through acetylation resulted in an increase in peak viscosity [2,7,9,10,47]. This increase can be attributed to several factors: i) The strength of associative intermolecular forces in the amorphous regions of the starch granules decreases after acetylation. This weakening of intermolecular forces allows for easier movement and interaction of starch molecules, leading to increased viscosity. ii) Acetylated starch samples were found to have lower degrees of crystallinity and a higher proportion of amorphous regions in the starch structure compared to native wheat starches. This reduced crystallinity contributes to enhanced swelling and solubility of the acetylated starch, leading to increased viscosity [46]. iii) The modification of starch through acetylation increases its swelling power, as depicted in the previous study conducted by Abedi et al. (2019), contributing to the increased viscosity observed [6]. iv) Acetylation results in the solubilization of amylose, which is released outside the swollen starch granules. This solubilized amylose contributes to the increased viscosity of the acetylated starch samples [47]. v) Acetylation also improves the hydrophilic capacity of the reorganized starch structures. This improved hydrophilic capacity further contributes to the increased viscosity observed [47].

The velocity contours and vectors are illustrated in Fig. 3 for the frequency of 25 kHz above the ultrasonic transducer at various time instances. Due to the tip surface oscillations of the transducer, several pressure waves are created and passing through the fluid to create an upward flow stream which transport energy to the examined beakers above them. As it is obvious, comparing the snap shots of the flow field, the creation of the upward stream, its propagation within the tank and its impact on the upper beaker can be observed from 0.081 to 6.581 s. Moreover, the flow jet from the vibrating transducer creates significant circulation in the domain.

The relationship between the intensity of an acoustic field and its frequency is determined by the unique attributes of the sound source and the surrounding environment. Generally, sound intensity measures the rate at which sound energy passes through a given area perpendicular to the direction of sound propagation. It can be expressed mathematically as the square of the sound pressure. Typically, the intensity of a sound wave varies across different frequencies. As the transducer’s frequency increases for a constant amplitude, the amount energy delivered to the fluid increases. Therefore, the enhancement of the acoustic intensity with transducers frequency has a solid physical interpretation.

The results for the acoustic simulation are depicted in Fig. 4 including the total power dissipation density and surface intensity magnitude for frequencies of 25 and 40 kHz. The total power dissipation has direct influence on the amount of energy release in the flow field. Sonication frequency increase alters the distribution of the dissipated power density and its magnitude. According to Fig. 4-a, for higher frequency, the density is less diffused in the domain and its maximum value increases from 8.97
×
104 to 2.51
×
105 (179 % enhancement). Moreover, it is observed that the intensity magnitude is more targeted to the upper beaker for higher applied frequency (see Fig. 4-b). The maximum magnitude of acoustic intensity is enhanced from 5.27
×
103 to 1.51
×
104 (186 % increase) by increasing the applied frequency from 25 to 40 kHz.

The variation of shear stress and viscosity as a function of shear rate are revealed in Fig. 5 for normal and sonicated wheat starch. As it is obvious, a linear relation between the shear rate and shear stress doesn’t exist especially at low shear rate and consequently the flow is non-Newtonian. According to the viscosity chart (Fig. 5-a), the viscosity at very low shear rates (i.e., 0.1 to 1 s−1) has a considerable magnitude. In other words, similar to solid phase for which a shear stress is required at zero shear rate, the examined fluids are demonstrate to have yield shear stress. So, to model this rheology property, the Casson and Herschel–Bulkley viscosity models can be implemented.

Additionally, focusing on the shear rates higher than about 600 s−1, there is a linear relation between the shear rate and shear stress (see Fig. 5-b). Thus, the utilization of the Casson model to simulate this behavior is reasonable.

According to the results, the sonication treatment significantly enhances the flow viscosity, especially at low shear rates. The most effective treatment for viscosity enhancement is for 25 + 40 kHz frequency, followed by 25 and 40 kHz cases. The difference between various cases for shear rates higher than 100 is almost negligible.

The data for the power law rheology model are listed in Table 2 . The value of the flow index (n < 1) demonstrates a shear thinning nature. For the shear thinning flows, the viscosity is reduced by increasing the shear rate. Besides, the difference between n-value and unity is higher for normal wheat starch in comparison to sonicated starches, which implies the sonication treatment increases the Newtonian behavior. Among the sonicated cases, the highest flow index value (i.e., the most Newtonian properties), belongs to AC wheat starch (25 + 40 kHz). The assessment parameters for the power law model are above 0.98 for all of the examined cases which confirms the model compatibility with experimental data.

The parameters for the Herschel–Bulkley viscosity model are listed in Table 3 . This model is known as an expansion of the common power law model to account for the yield stress (
τ 0
)
. The consideration of the yield stress is useful for fluids that have a non-zero viscosity at very low shear rates (solid-like behavior). According to the obtained results, implementation of the sonication treatment, increases the yield shear stress between 24.1 and 31.8 %, based on the applied frequency. Moreover, the flow index (n), which represents the relation between the shear stress and shear rate is increased between 30.7 and 76.6 % by sonication. Regarding the compatibility of the Herschel–Bulkley model to the rheology data, it is observed that the values of R2 and Adj-R2 are higher than 0.99 for all cases.

The Casson model parameters are presented in Table 4 . The Casson model assumes yield stress and Newtonian behavior at low and high shear rates, respectively. Based on the obtained data, the viscosity at high shear rates (
η C
) is increased by sonication process in comparison to normal starch, however the predicted viscosity value for 25 + 40 kHz is significantly higher than the other sonication cases. Moreover, in contrary to the Herschel–Bulkley model for which a meaningful effect of sonication on the yield shear stress is obvious, the
τ 0
value is varied between 2.126 and 3.96 Pa based on Casson model.

Based on DSC gelatinization parameters, (To, Tp, and Tc) and the enthalpy of gelatinization (ΔH gel), were lower in AC starches at the frequency of 25 + 40 kHz compared to AC starches at frequencies of 25 kHz and 40 kHz, as well as N starches. For instance, the gelatinization temperature decreased from 56.8 ± 0.8 °C for N wheat starch to 48.2 ± 1.04 °C, 51.0 ± 0.8 °C, and 44.9 ± 1.4 °C for AC wheat starches at frequencies of 25 kHz, 40 kHz, and 25 + 40 kHz, respectively. This decrease in gelatinization temperature suggests that the modification process, particularly at the frequency of 25 + 40 kHz, affected the thermal properties of the starch and resulted in a lower temperature required for gelatinization. Additionally, the enthalpy of gelatinization was also influenced by the modification process. However, the specific values for the enthalpy were not provided in the statement. Nonetheless, it can be inferred that the enthalpy of gelatinization was lower in AC starches compared to N starches, indicating a reduction in the energy required for gelatinization.

ΔHgel values varied among the different starches. The NS starch exhibited the highest ΔHgel value of 11.8 ± 0.8 J/g. In comparison, ACs at frequencies of 25 kHz, 40 kHz, and 25 + 40 kHz had lower ΔHgel values of 6.4 ± 0.8 J/g, 9.5 ± 0.4 J/g, and 5.8 ± 0.8 J/g, respectively. Starch gelatinization is influenced by numerous main factors mainly the molecular structure of amylopectin and the structure of starch granules. The amylopectin molecular structure includes parameters such as molar mass, crystallite order, polydispersity, and the length of amylopectin chains with a degree of polymerization ranging between 5 and 12. The granule structure is determined by factors such as granule size, the ratio of amylose to amylopectin, the relationship between crystalline zones, and the presence of amorphous crystallites [6,48,49]. The enthalpy value associated with the loss of ordered double helices during gelatinization is higher than the loss of crystallinity. Comparing the enthalpy values of NS and AC indicated that the acetylation of wheat starch with a high degree of substitution leads to disorganization, as observed through SEM, and RVA experiments. The reduced gelatinization parameters of acetylated starches can be attributed to the introduction of acetyl oxide groups into the starch molecules, particularly in the amorphous regions. This disrupts the inter- and intra-molecular hydrogen bonds, resulting in a decrease in the integrity of both the amorphous and crystalline sections of starch granules and destabilizing the granular structure. The ΔH value provides an overall measure of crystallinity and the loss of molecular order. Esterifying agents, such as those used in acetylation, reduce the ΔH value by modifying the amylopectin double helices, diminishing crystallinity, increasing starch chain flexibility, and reducing the gelatinization temperature [38]. The results obtained in this study are in line with the findings reported by Halal et al. (2015), Bello-Pérez et al. (2010), and Mirmoghtadaie et al. (2009) [7,38,39].

The degree of gelatinization as well as the reaction rate as a function of temperature are illustrated in Fig. 6 . According to the obtained results, the gelatinization trend is almost the same for all of the examined cased including the effect of sonication and AC addition (Fig. 6-a). The gelatinization is started with high rate at initial stages, followed by nearly constant rate and a low-rate reaction at final stage (near complete gelatinization, α = 100 %). However, the implementation of sonication treatment reduced the initiation temperature of gelatinization significantly (i.e., 8.6, 5.8 and 11.9 °C reduction for AC-25 kHz, AC-40 kHz and AC-25 + 40 kHz, respectively comparted to normal starch). Besides, it is observed that for each case, by increasing the temperature in the DSC device the reaction rate is increased exponentially until the completion of the gelatinization.

To examine the effect of sonication and AWS on the gelatinization, the non-isothermal kinetic study is performed based on the DSC data. The gelatinization degree as a function of temperature is used to determine the reaction kinetics by implementation of multiple linear regression. The activation energy, pre-exponent coefficient and reaction order as well the regression assessment parameters are obtained and listed in Table 5 .

Based on the values of R2 and adj-R2 it can be concluded that the assumption of nth-order reaction rate is valid and the assumed model is compatible to the obtained results with sufficient accuracy. According to the results, the activation energy as a barrier for higher rate chemical reactions, is reduced significantly by utilization of the sonication treatment. For instance, implementation of 25 kHz sonication reduces the activation energy by 70.3 %. However, the frequency of the ultrasound waves or the addition of AC during sonication process has slighter effect on the activation energy (i.e., 10.4 % variation of Ea ). Regarding the value of the reaction order (n), it can be concluded that in all of the examined cases, a similar value around 0.85 is obtained. The reaction kinetics rare order is determined based on the mathematical fitting. The reaction order is calculated to be below unity for all of the examined cases. The physical interpretation of the calculated reaction order emphasizes on the fact the reaction rate is less dependent to the gelatinization progress than a common 1st-order reaction kinetics.

It's important to note that both the Herschel–Bulkley and Casson fluid models are derived under the assumption of the existence of shear yield stress. However, it is worth mentioning that the mathematical formulations of these two models are different. In both the Herschel–Bulkley and Casson models, the relationship between the shear stress and the yield shear stress is the same for very low and very high shear rates. This means that at extremely low or extremely high shear rates, the behavior of the fluid is similar in both models. However, at moderate shear rates, the effect of both the shear yield stress and the shear rate becomes significant. As a result, when fitting the experimental data to these models, we may obtain different values for the shear yield stress. In conclusion, while both the Herschel–Bulkley and Casson models account for the presence of shear yield stress, the difference lies in their mathematical formulations, leading to varying values for shear yield stress at moderate shear rates.

The benefits offered by modified starches make them valuable ingredients in various industries. Acetylation of the starch has several beneficial effects on its properties. It improves solubility, water absorption, resistance to freeze and thaw cycles, and viscosity peak. Additionally, acetylation reduces the gelatinization temperature of the starch. To enhance the acetylation efficiency, the acetylation mixture was conducted in the presence of sonication. The use of ultrasonic processes in the reaction mixture allowed for an increase in DS while minimizing the amount of reagents required. The optimized molar substitutions for ACs was found to be 1.22 %, 1.37 %, and 1.02 % for ultrasonic frequencies of 25 kHz, 25 + 40 kHz, and 40 kHz, respectively. These DS values were achieved using only 5 % acetic anhydride. The high DS obtained under the dual frequency ultrasonic conditions (25 + 40 kHz) can be attributed to the formation of fractures, pores, and cracks on the surface of the starch granules. These structural modifications facilitate the penetration of the acetic anhydride reagent into the granules, leading to a higher degree of substitution. In other words, at lower frequencies, the collapse of the cavitational bubbles is more violent, leading to increased agitation and enhanced mass transfer. The maximum magnitude of acoustic intensity is enhanced from 5.27
×
103 to 1.51
×
104 (186 % increase) by increasing the applied frequency from 25 to 40 kHz. Based on the CFD simulation, the creation of the upward stream, its propagation within the tank and its impact on the upper beaker can be observed from 0.081 to 6.581 s. Utilization of sonication treatment reduced the initiation temperature of gelatinization significantly (i.e., 8.6, 5.8 and 11.9 °C reduction for AC-25 kHz, AC-40 kHz and AC-25 + 40 kHz, respectively comparted to normal starch). Implementation of 25 kHz sonication reduces the activation energy by 70.3 %. However, the frequency of the ultrasound waves or the addition of AC during sonication process has slighter effect on the activation energy (i.e., 10.4 % variation of Ea ). The reduction of activation energy is obtained to be 69.9 % and 67.1 % for application of 40 and 25 + 40 kHz transducers. By increasing the temperature in the DSC device, the reaction rate is increased exponentially until the completion of the gelatinization. During the sonication treatment, the yield shear stress increases between 24.1 and 31.8 %, based on the applied frequency.

Elahe Abedi and Shima Kaveh wrote the manuscript, conceived and designed the research and analyzed the data. Seyed Mohammad Bagher Hashemi conceived and designed the research and analyzed the data. Elahe Abedi and Shima Kaveh conducted the experiments. All the authors read and approved the manuscript.
Research data are not shared.
Not applicable.
The present paper has been approved by all named authors.
The present paper, which is original, has not been published before and is not currently being considered for publication elsewhere.
This article does not contain any studies with human participants or animals performed by any of the authors.
The authors declare that they have no conflict of interest.

Elahe Abedi: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Reza Roohi: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Seyed Mohammad Bagher Hashemi: . Shima Kaveh: Validation, Software, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
